data.title: The Elmet Group Co. GAAP EPS of -$0.02, revenue of $56M
data.description:
• The Elmet Group Co. press release (ELMT): Q1 GAAP EPS of -$0.02.
  • Revenue of $56M.
  • Gross profit margin improved 260 basis points to 21.2% of revenue compared to 18.6% of revenue Q1 2025.
  • Adjusted EBITDA increased to approximately $9.2 million, or 16.4% of revenue, compared to approximately $4.5 million, or 9.6% of revenue, in Q1 2025.
  • Open order backlog increased to approximately $113.3 million, up from approximately $96.3 million at the end of Q4 2025 and approximately $74.7 million at the end of Q1 2025.

data.title: Castlelake confirms early stage interest in offer for easyJet (update)
data.description:
Update 3:55pm: Adds company comment.

EasyJet (EJTTF) rose 1% in London trading amid takeover speculation. Castlelake confirmed that it's in the early stages of considering a possible offer for the airline.

No approach has been made to the board of easyJet, Castlelake said in a statement on Friday. Under UK rules, Castlelake has to announce by June 26 if it will make a firm intention to make an offer or won't make a bid. 

Earlier on Friday, a Betaville "uncooked" alert said takeover interest for easyJet was coming from a private equity firm working with an aircraft leasing company.

data.title: Cerebras continues to plunge; shares now down 24% from opening day close
data.description:
Cerebras Systems (CBRS) hit the market with a splash, with shares surging nearly 70% by the time markets closed on the day of its initial public offering on May 14. It marked the biggest listing of the year.

Shares were trading at $385 just moments after going live. However, they've since taken a tumble and are down roughly 25% from their opening day close of $311. Still, the stock remains above its IPO price of $185.

The impressive start allowed Cerebras to receive a fast-track authorization for inclusion on the S&P Dow Jones Indices.  

The company's flagship technology includes the Wafer-Scale Engine 3, or WSE-3, which it contends is the fastest commercialized AI processor in the world. The company said it is 58 times larger than the leading GPU chip and uses a fraction of the power per unit of compute while delivering inference up to 15 times faster than leading GPU-based solutions offered by competitor Nvidia (NVDA).

Last month, Cerebras secured a $20B deal with OpenAI (OPENAI) to provide servers powered by its AI chips over the next three years. And in March, it announced a collaboration with Amazon (AMZN) to deploy a new AI data center solution designed to increase inference speed. It has a backlog of $24.6B. The vast majority of that is tied to OpenAI. Cerebras expects to recognize 15% of that amount within the next two years.

"Based on the partnership with OpenAI/AWS and our analysis on TAM, we expect its revenue to be at the $1.2B, $3.2B, and $5.5B levels in 2026, 2027E, and 2028E, suggesting 10x 2028E P/S at current level," said GF Securities analyst Jeff Pu. "That said, the valuation doesn't seem particularly cheap, while the upside risks would be the breakthrough in scaling."

data.title: Halliburton in sync with peers' monthly downtrend: Why is HAL price dropping?
data.description:
Halliburton (HAL) has been under pressure since the past month, with the stock falling more than ~7% to ~$38.88, while the S&P 500 (SP500) gained ~6.46% during the same period.

Even after posting higher profit in Q1 2026, investors remained focused on weaker business trends underneath the headline numbers.

Halliburton’s Q1 revenue stayed nearly flat at $5.4B, showing the company is struggling to grow despite strong energy market expectations. 

The Middle East/Asia business was another concern, with revenue falling 13% YoY to $1.3B due to lower activity in Saudi Arabia and Qatar. The company also said geopolitical tensions reduced earnings by nearly $0.02-$0.03 per share.

Its important Completion & Production segment also disappointed investors. Revenue fell 3% to $3B, while operating income dropped 17% to $439M as North American activity slowed and Middle East sales weakened.

Even though Barclays upgraded Halliburton (HAL) to overweight with a $55 price target after results, investors are still worried about weak cash flow, slower shale recovery, and margin pressure.

At the same time, lower oil prices and uncertainty linked to the US-Iran conflict have also hurt sentiment across energy stocks. However, recent investments in VoltaGrid and stable dividends have provided limited support so far.

Peer weakness is also visible. Other energy stock names, including SLB N.V. (SLB), Baker Hughes (BKR), BP (BP), Exxon Mobil (XOM), NOV (NOV), Equinor (EQNR), Shell (SHEL), TechnipFMC (FTI), and Weatherford International (WFRD), have also traded lower over the past month.

data.title: Canada’s economy: the parrot has croaked
data.description:
Canada (EWC) on Friday edged into a technical recession as weak business and government spending drove a slight contraction in the first quarter, signaling persistent slack in the economy in the wake of U.S. trade tensions.

Real gross domestic product declined 0.1% on an annualized basis during the first three months of 2026, Statistics Canada reported Friday. The contraction comes after a 1% drop in the fourth quarter—revised downward from the 0.6% decrease the federal agency had previously reported.

The first-quarter decline caught forecasters off guard. Economists surveyed by Bloomberg had expected a 1.5% annualized increase, in line with the Bank of Canada’s own projection.

Canada last recorded two consecutive quarters of negative growth during the Covid-19 pandemic in 2020. Before that, the country experienced a similar downturn in 2015 when oil (USO) (BNO) prices collapsed.

James Thorne, chief market strategist at Wellington-Altus, offered a blistering assessment of the news, dubbing Canada’s (EWC) situation the “parrot economy” and criticizing what he sees as institutional denial about the nation's economic trajectory.

“Canada has slipped into a technical recession, yet Bay Street pundits and the Bank of Canada continue to insist the economy is ‘resilient’ and capable of absorbing further rate hikes. This is not analysis, it’s denial,” Thorne said.

Drawing a comparison to Monty Python’s famous “dead parrot” sketch, Thorne argued that officials are engaged in “semantic gymnastics” while fundamental economic data deteriorates.

“The economy isn’t weakening, it’s ‘normalizing’; it isn’t rolling over, it’s ‘adjusting,’” he said, characterizing the official narrative. “At some point, this stops being a difference of interpretation and starts looking like institutional groupthink.”

Thorne warned that the insistence on economic strength in the face of weakening fundamentals risks a policy error—one where rates remain too restrictive for too long, deepening the downturn that policymakers claim does not exist.

“The parrot is not resting,” Thorne said. “It is, unmistakably, dead.”
